Press enter or click to view image in full size

The morning I finally deleted forty thousand lines of React, my hands were shaking on the keyboard.
Not from fear. From something closer to relief mixed with grief, the way you feel packing up an apartment you were never quite happy in.
Two years earlier, I had inherited a single page application built by people who knew what they were doing.
Dashboards, forms, a notification system, all wired together with React, Redux, and a state management layer that had grown so many opinions it needed its own documentation just to explain itself to new hires.
If you have ever stared at a component tree at night, trying to find the one effect that is quietly re-rendering a screen it has no business touching, you already know this story. You have lived a version of it yourself.
The Job Nobody Warned Me About
My title was frontend engineer. My actual job, most weeks, was closer to archaeologist.
I spent entire afternoons digging through layers of context providers, trying to understand why a checkbox on one page was somehow affecting a chart on another.
None of this was because the people before me were careless. They were sharp, thoughtful…
